hypothesis: UCB1-weighted dispatch produces higher quality than equal-weight allocation. Expert weighting justifies concentration.
method: Compared UCB1-weighted (proportional to actual lane allocation) vs equal-weight (each domain weighted equally) across 54 lanes in 12 domains with >=2 lanes. Bootstrap n=5000 for significance. Quality metrics: merge rate, self-rated Sharpe, L3+ fraction.
expect: UCB1 wins on mean quality metrics. Equal-weight wins on diversity/discovery.
actual: NO SIGNIFICANT DIFFERENCE on any metric. Equal-weight merge +7.6pp (CI [-0.8, +15.4]). UCB1 Sharpe +0.58 (CI [-0.14, +1.42]). Top-3 domains 48.1% of lanes but 20.9pp LOWER merge rate. Thin domains merge at 86.1% vs thick at 36.4%.
diff: Expected UCB1 wins mean: NOT FOUND. Expected equal-weight wins diversity: PARTIALLY confirmed (thin > thick). Surprise: concentration HURTS merge rate.
verdict: PARTIALLY CONFIRMED — UCB1 does not significantly outperform random equal allocation. Concentration correlates negatively with merge rate. Mediocrity selection operates at the dispatch level: UCB1 concentrates into high-volume low-quality domains.
